docs.title: 1. Overview
docs.text:
RepX is built to evaluate representation quality directly from embeddings. If you can produce a representation tensor, you can analyze it with RepX.

This works across learning paradigms:

  • Supervised learning (classification, regression backbones)
  • Self-supervised learning (contrastive, masked modeling, distillation)
  • Unsupervised learning (autoencoders, clustering-based pipelines)

The only requirement is a representation, e.g. z = model(x).

docs.title: 2. Installation
docs.text: pip install repx

docs.title: 3. Examples
docs.text:
Start from the same pattern in every setup:

# model can be any feature extractor: CNN, ViT, MLP, SSL encoder, etc.
z = model(x)

docs.title: Transfer (downstream performance)
docs.text:
import torch
from repx.transfer import LinearProbeEvaluator, NCCCEvaluator

# model can be any model that outputs representations
z_train = model(x_train)
z_test = model(x_test)

# Linear probe: train a linear classifier on frozen features
lp = LinearProbeEvaluator(device="cpu")
train_acc, test_acc = lp.evaluate(
    train_features=z_train,
    train_labels=y_train,
    test_features=z_test,
    test_labels=y_test,
    num_output_classes=num_classes,
    n_shots=None,  # set to int for few-shot per class
)

# NCCC: nearest class center evaluation
nccc = NCCCEvaluator(device="cpu")
accs = nccc.evaluate(
    features=z_test,
    labels=y_test,
    n_shots=5,
    repeat=3,
)

docs.title: Geometry (collapse analysis)
docs.text:
from repx.collapse import compute_cdnv, compute_directional_cdnv

# z can come from any model
z = model(x)

cdnv = compute_cdnv(z, y, num_classes=num_classes)
dir_cdnv = compute_directional_cdnv(z, y, num_classes=num_classes)

docs.title: Alignment (representation similarity)
docs.text:
from repx import RSA, CKA

z_a = model_a(x)
z_b = model_b(x)

rsa = RSA(rdm_metric="correlation", compare="spearman")
cka = CKA(kernel="linear")

print(rsa.rsa(z_a, z_b).item())
print(cka.cka(z_a, z_b).item())

For full API docs, see Alignment, Transfer, Core, and Collapse.

docs.title: 4. Contributing
docs.text: See CONTRIBUTING.md for contribution guidelines and development workflow.

docs.title: 5. License
docs.text: RepX is released under the GNU GPLv3. See LICENSE.md for details.

docs.title: Contributing
docs.text:
Thanks for your interest in contributing to RepX.

This project aims to provide clean, reliable tools for evaluating learned representations in PyTorch.

docs.title: 1. Clone the repository
docs.text:
git clone https://github.com/nishit/repx.git
cd repx

docs.title: 2. Set up a virtual environment
docs.text:
conda create -n repx-dev python=3.10
conda activate repx-dev
pip install -e ".[dev]"

docs.title: 3. Run tests to verify your setup
docs.text: pytest tests/

docs.title: 1. Before writing code
docs.text:
• Check existing structure (alignment/, collapse/, transfer/)
  • Follow the same style and API patterns
  • Keep functions simple and modular

docs.title: 2. Adding a new metric (example: CKA)
docs.text:
1. Create a new file in the appropriate submodule:
src/repx/alignment/cka.py
  1. Implement the metric.
  2. Add tests and run them.
  3. Export it in src/repx/__init__.py if part of the public API.
  4. Update documentation.

docs.title: Testing
docs.text: • Use pytest for testing.

docs.title: 1. Before submitting a PR
docs.text:
• Run tests: pytest tests/
  • Run hooks: pre-commit run --all-files
  • Make sure CI passes

docs.title: 2. PRs should
docs.text:
• Be focused (one feature or bugfix per PR)
  • Include tests for new features or bugfixes
  • Follow existing code style

docs.title: CKA
docs.text:
Centered Kernel Alignment in PyTorch.

Compares two representation spaces with centered kernel alignment using the debiased HSIC estimator.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default kernel str

Kernel used to build the similarity matrix.

"linear" (default) K = X @ Xᵀ. Invariant to orthogonal transformations and isotropic scaling of the rows.

'linear' Notes
  • Invariant to orthogonal transforms and isotropic scaling with linear kernel.
  • Requires at least 4 stimuli (rows).

docs.title: compute_kernel(X)
docs.text:
Compute the kernel (Gram) matrix for a set of representations.

Examples:

import torch
from repx.alignment import CKA

X = torch.randn(4, 3)
K = CKA().compute_kernel(X)
print(K.shape)  # torch.Size([4, 4])

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default X (Tensor, shape(n_samples, n_features))

One row per stimulus.

required

Returns:

Name Type Description K (Tensor, shape(n_samples, n_samples))

Symmetric positive semi-definite kernel matrix.

docs.title: cka(X, Y)
docs.text:
Compute Centered Kernel Alignment (CKA).

Builds a kernel matrix for each representation, zeros the diagonals, and computes the normalised debiased HSIC.

Requires at least 4 stimuli (rows) due to the n*(n-3) denominator in the unbiased HSIC estimator.

Examples:

import torch
from repx.alignment import CKA

cka = CKA(kernel="linear")
X = torch.randn(20, 64)
Y = torch.randn(20, 128)

score = cka.cka(X, Y)
print(score.shape)  # torch.Size([])

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default X Tensor, shape (n_samples, n_features_*)

Row-per-sample matrices. Both must have the same number of rows; feature dimensionalities may differ.

required Y Tensor, shape (n_samples, n_features_*)

Row-per-sample matrices. Both must have the same number of rows; feature dimensionalities may differ.

required

Returns:

Name Type Description score scalar Tensor

CKA score. Returns 1.0 for identical representations, ≈ 0.0 for independent ones. Call .item() for a Python float.

Raises:

Type Description ValueError

If X and Y have different number of samples or n < 4.

docs.title: RSA
docs.text:
Representational Similarity Analysis in PyTorch.

Compares two representation spaces by correlating their Representational Dissimilarity Matrices (RDMs).

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default rdm_metric ('correlation', 'cosine', 'euclidean', 'cityblock')

Pairwise distance metric used when building RDMs. Options: "correlation" (1 − Pearson r), "cosine" (1 − cosine similarity), "euclidean" (L2 distance), and "cityblock" (L1/Manhattan distance).

"correlation" compare ('spearman', 'pearson')

Correlation method used when comparing RDM vectors.

"spearman" Notes
  • Identical representations produce scores near 1.
  • Independent representations usually produce scores near 0.

docs.title: compute_rdm(X)
docs.text:
Compute the Representational Dissimilarity Matrix (RDM).

Examples:

import torch
from repx.alignment import RSA

X = torch.randn(5, 3)
rdm = RSA(rdm_metric="correlation").compute_rdm(X)
print(rdm.shape)  # torch.Size([5, 5])

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default X (Tensor, shape(n_samples, n_features))

One row per sample.

required

Returns:

Name Type Description rdm (Tensor, shape(n_samples, n_samples))

Symmetric matrix with zero diagonal.

docs.title: rdm_upper_tri(rdm)
docs.text:
Extract the strict upper-triangle of an RDM as a flat 1-D vector.

Examples:

import torch
from repx.alignment import RSA

rdm = torch.tensor(
    [[0.0, 1.0, 2.0], [1.0, 0.0, 3.0], [2.0, 3.0, 0.0]]
)
vec = RSA().rdm_upper_tri(rdm)
print(vec)  # tensor([1., 2., 3.])

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default rdm (Tensor, shape(n, n)) required

Returns:

Name Type Description vec (Tensor, shape(n * (n - 1) // 2))

docs.title: rsa(X, Y)
docs.text:
Compute Representational Similarity Analysis (RSA).

Builds an RDM for each representation matrix, vectorises the upper triangles, and correlates them using the settings from __init__.

Examples:

import torch
from repx.alignment import RSA

rsa = RSA(rdm_metric="correlation", compare="spearman")
X = torch.randn(20, 64)
Y = torch.randn(20, 128)

score = rsa.rsa(X, Y)
print(score.shape)  # torch.Size([])

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default X Tensor, shape (n_samples, n_features_*)

Row-per-sample matrices. Both must have the same number of rows; feature dimensionalities may differ.

required Y Tensor, shape (n_samples, n_features_*)

Row-per-sample matrices. Both must have the same number of rows; feature dimensionalities may differ.

required

Returns:

Name Type Description r scalar Tensor

Correlation coefficient in [−1, +1]. Call .item() to get a Python float.

Raises:

Type Description ValueError

If X and Y have different number of samples.

docs.title: Alignment
docs.text:
Alignment-based representation metrics.

Alignment metrics compare representation spaces from one or more models.

  • RSA measures whether pairwise similarity structure between examples is preserved across representations.
  • CKA measures global alignment between representation spaces in a way that is robust to common linear transformations.

Use these metrics when you want to compare learned representations from different models, layers, checkpoints, or training regimes.

See:

  • RSA
  • CKA

docs.title: compute_cdnv
docs.text:
Compute class-distance normalized variance (CDNV).

For each unordered class pair (c1, c2), this computes:

  • class variance proxy: E[||x||^2] - ||E[x]||^2 for each class,
  • average variance across the two classes,
  • normalized by squared distance between class means.

The final result is averaged over num_classes * (num_classes - 1) / 2 possible class pairs.

Examples:

import torch
from repx.collapse import compute_cdnv

x = torch.randn(100, 128)
y = torch.randint(0, 10, (100,))

score = compute_cdnv(x, y, num_classes=10)
print(score)

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default features (Tensor, shape(num_samples, feature_dim))

Embedding matrix.

required labels (Tensor, shape(num_samples))

Class labels for each sample.

required num_classes int

Number of classes. If omitted, inferred as labels.max() + 1.

None device str or device

Device used for metric computation.

"cpu" eps float

Small positive value to avoid division by zero for degenerate class pairs.

1e-12

Returns:

Type Description float

Average CDNV value.

docs.title: compute_directional_cdnv
docs.text:
Compute directional CDNV.

Directional CDNV measures, for ordered class pairs (c1, c2), the variance of class-c1 features projected onto the direction from mean(c1) to mean(c2), normalized by squared inter-class distance.

Examples:

import torch
from repx.collapse import compute_directional_cdnv

x = torch.randn(100, 128)
y = torch.randint(0, 10, (100,))

score = compute_directional_cdnv(x, y, num_classes=10)
print(score)

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default features (Tensor, shape(num_samples, feature_dim))

Embedding matrix.

required labels (Tensor, shape(num_samples))

Class labels for each sample.

required means (Tensor, shape(num_classes, feature_dim))

Precomputed class means. If omitted, class means are computed from features and labels.

None num_classes int

Number of classes. If omitted, inferred as labels.max() + 1.

None device str or device

Device used for metric computation.

"cpu" eps float

Small positive value to avoid division by zero for degenerate class directions.

1e-12

Returns:

Type Description float

Average directional CDNV value over ordered class pairs.

docs.title: compute_etf_deviation
docs.text:
Compute deviation of class means from simplex ETF geometry.

The metric is based on class means. It first centers class means across active classes and L2-normalizes each mean vector. It then compares the resulting Gram matrix to the ETF target Gram matrix:

  • diagonal entries = 1
  • off-diagonal entries = -1 / (C - 1)

where C is the number of active classes (classes that appear in labels). Returned value is the relative Frobenius error:

||G - G_etf||_F / ||G_etf||_F

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default features (Tensor, shape(num_samples, feature_dim))

Embedding matrix.

required labels (Tensor, shape(num_samples))

Class labels for each sample.

required num_classes int

Total number of classes. If omitted, inferred as labels.max() + 1.

None device str or device

Device used for metric computation.

"cpu" eps float

Numerical stability constant used in vector normalization.

1e-12

Returns:

Type Description float

Relative ETF deviation. Lower is better; 0 indicates perfect ETF geometry for active class means.

docs.title: Collapse
docs.text:
Collapse-based representation metrics.

Collapse metrics characterize representation geometry and class-structure behavior.

  • CDNV measures class variance normalized by inter-class distance.
  • Directional CDNV measures variance along class-to-class directions.
  • ETF Deviation measures distance from simplex ETF geometry.

See:

  • compute_cdnv
  • compute_directional_cdnv
  • compute_etf_deviation

docs.title: Core
docs.text:
Shared utilities and base classes.

OverviewSubmodulesFiles

repx.core — Shared utilities and base classes.

No submodules are defined yet.

  • src/repx/core/__init__.py

docs.title: LinearProbeEvaluator
docs.text:
Train and evaluate a linear probe on frozen features.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default device str or device

Device used for training and evaluation.

"cpu"

docs.title: evaluate
docs.text:
Train and evaluate a linear probe.

Examples:

import torch
from repx.transfer import LinearProbeEvaluator

z_train = torch.randn(256, 128)
y_train = torch.randint(0, 10, (256,))
z_test = torch.randn(128, 128)
y_test = torch.randint(0, 10, (128,))

evaluator = LinearProbeEvaluator(device="cpu")
train_acc, test_acc = evaluator.evaluate(
    train_features=z_train,
    train_labels=y_train,
    test_features=z_test,
    test_labels=y_test,
    num_output_classes=10,
    n_shots=5,
    repeat=3,
)

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default train_features (Tensor, shape(num_train, feature_dim))

Frozen train embeddings.

required train_labels (Tensor, shape(num_train))

Train labels.

required test_features (Tensor, shape(num_test, feature_dim))

Frozen test embeddings.

required test_labels (Tensor, shape(num_test))

Test labels.

required num_output_classes int

Number of output classes for the linear probe layer.

required lr float

Learning rate for Adam.

3e-4 epochs int

Number of optimization epochs.

100 n_shots int

Number of samples per class for few-shot training. If omitted, full filtered train data is used.

None repeat int

Number of repeated runs.

5 selected_classes sequence[int]

If provided, restrict training/evaluation to this class subset.

None optimizer str or Optimizer class

Optimizer choice used to train the probe.

"adam" optimizer_kwargs mapping

Extra keyword arguments passed to optimizer constructor. If lr is not provided, this method's lr argument is used.

None loss_fn str or nn.Module class

Loss function used for probe training.

"cross_entropy" loss_fn_kwargs mapping

Extra keyword arguments passed to the loss constructor.

None

Returns:

Type Description tuple[float, float]

(mean_train_accuracy, mean_test_accuracy).

docs.title: NCCCEvaluator
docs.text:
Nearest Class Center Classifier (NCCC) evaluator.

Computes class centers from support representations and evaluates classification by nearest center in Euclidean space.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default device str or device

Device used for computation.

"cpu"

docs.title: compute_class_centers
docs.text:
Compute class centers from full- or few-shot support data.

Examples:

import torch
from repx.transfer import NCCCEvaluator

features = torch.randn(100, 64)
labels = torch.randint(0, 10, (100,))

nccc = NCCCEvaluator(device="cpu")
centers_list, selected = nccc.compute_class_centers(
    features,
    labels,
    n_shots=5,
    repeat=3,
)

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default features (Tensor, shape(num_samples, feature_dim))

Support feature matrix.

required labels (Tensor, shape(num_samples))

Support labels.

required n_shots int

Number of support samples per class. If omitted, use all support samples in the selected subset.

None repeat int

Number of repeated center computations when n_shots is set.

1 selected_classes sequence[int]

If provided, restrict center computation to this class subset.

None

Returns:

Type Description tuple[list[Tensor], list[int]]

(centers_per_repeat, resolved_classes) where each centers tensor has shape (num_selected_classes, feature_dim).

docs.title: evaluate
docs.text:
Evaluate accuracy using nearest class centers.

Centers are computed internally by :meth:compute_class_centers from the same input features and labels.

Examples:

import torch
from repx.transfer import NCCCEvaluator

features = torch.randn(100, 64)
labels = torch.randint(0, 10, (100,))

nccc = NCCCEvaluator(device="cpu")
accs = nccc.evaluate(features, labels, n_shots=5, repeat=3)
print(accs)

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default features (Tensor, shape(num_samples, feature_dim))

Query feature matrix.

required labels (Tensor, shape(num_samples))

Query labels.

required n_shots int

Number of support samples per class used to compute centers.

None repeat int

Number of repeated evaluations when n_shots is set.

1 selected_classes sequence[int]

If provided, evaluate only this class subset.

None

Returns:

Type Description list[float]

Accuracy for each repeat.

docs.title: Transfer
docs.text:
Transfer-based representation metrics.

Transfer metrics measure how useful learned representations are for downstream tasks.

  • Linear Probe evaluates linear separability of representations.
  • NCCC evaluates classification quality using nearest class centers.

See:

  • LinearProbeEvaluator
  • NCCCEvaluator
